### The Role of Firewalls in Network Security
Firewalls are critical for network security, acting as barriers between internal networks and external threats. They control incoming and outgoing traffic, protecting against unauthorized access and cyber attacks. However, configuring firewalls effectively can be challenging, especially in large organizations.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5b8dbedc-2d3a-4187-acb5-f7d90f2921b4)

parts.content: Evaluate the role of firewalls in securing organizational networks, considering their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation.
parts.markscheme:
Firewalls play a crucial role in securing organizational networks by acting as a barrier between trusted and untrusted networks, controlling the flow of traffic based on predefined security rules. Their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation can be evaluated as follows:

- Firewalls provide a first line of defense against unauthorized access and malicious attacks by:
  - Filtering incoming and outgoing network traffic based on IP addresses, ports, and protocols <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Blocking known malicious traffic and preventing unauthorized access to internal resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Enforcing security policies and access control lists (ACLs) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Effectiveness of firewalls:
  - They can effectively prevent many types of network-based attacks, such as distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, port scans, and unauthorized access attempts <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls can log and monitor network traffic, providing valuable information for security analysis and incident response <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - They can be configured to allow or deny specific types of traffic, providing granular control over network access <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Potential challenges in implementation:
  - Configuring and maintaining firewalls can be complex, especially in large organizations with diverse network architectures and security requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls may not be effective against advanced threats, such as zero-day attacks, encrypted traffic, or insider threats <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Improper configuration or rule management can lead to security vulnerabilities or unintended network disruptions <marks>1 mark</marks>
  <commonMistake>Relying solely on firewalls as the primary security measure, neglecting other security controls like intrusion detection/prevention systems, antivirus software, and user awareness training</commonMistake> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Discuss the importance of regularly updating firewall rules and software to address emerging threats and vulnerabilities</examTip> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of firewalls, their capabilities, limitations, and the need for a multi-layered security approach</note>

Malicious software (malware) is a significant threat to users of personal devices, as it can steal sensitive information, disrupt services, or even cause financial losses. With increased connectivity, devices are more vulnerable to these attacks, raising ethical questions about responsibility in cybersecurity.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/64f48a50-a36c-41f4-8754-bc67eba14a5b)

### Software developers

- Conduct thorough testing and quality assurance to identify and fix vulnerabilities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide regular software updates and patches to address emerging threats <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adhere to ethical coding practices and industry standards for secure software development <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Educate users on safe computing practices and the risks of malware <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Users

questions.specification:
### Source A: Types of Computing Networks and Their Applications
Computing networks enable connectivity across different scales and purposes, from Local Area Networks (LANs) within buildings to Wide Area Networks (WANs) spanning cities. Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) enhance security, while Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer (P2P) architectures support various communication methods.

For instance, a VPN can secure remote employee access to a company’s intranet, and LANs connect devices within a corporate office to share resources efficiently.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7a3c6885-2c31-455f-b01e-7eb64e6d9ed2)

### Source B: Essential Components of Computing Networks
The effectiveness of networks depends on critical components such as clients, servers, routers, and switches. Modems connect devices to the internet, while network interfaces enable devices to join a network. These elements, together with wired and wireless media, make data transmission feasible across distances.

For example, a modem connects a home router to an internet service provider (ISP), enabling users to access the internet and local network resources.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d7a75ec2-df64-479b-af82-b9a6e19c859e)

### Source C: Transmission Media in Networks: Wired vs. Wireless
Networks rely on transmission media for data transfer. Wired media (e.g., Ethernet and fiber-optic cables) provide high-speed, reliable connections, while wireless media (e.g., Wi-Fi and cellular networks) enable mobility. Each medium has specific advantages depending on the application.

For instance, fiber-optic cables support high-speed internet in metropolitan areas, while Wi-Fi enables device connectivity within homes and offices without physical cabling.


### Source D: Network Protocols and Communication Rules
Protocols define communication standards for data exchange across networks. TCP/IP is foundational for the internet, while HTTP supports web browsing. Protocols like FTP and SMTP enable file transfers and email communication, respectively.

For example, TCP/IP underpins internet connections, ensuring data packets are transmitted between devices without data loss, even over long distances.

parts.content: With reference to Source A, identify two types of computing networks and describe one specific application for each.
parts.markscheme:
### Types of networks (award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each type identified, max <marks>2 marks</marks>):

- LAN (Local Area Network)
- WAN (Wide Area Network)
- PAN (Personal Area Network)
- MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)
- VPN (Virtual Private Network)

### Application for each type (award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each valid application described, max <marks>2 marks</marks>):

- LAN: Used to connect computers/devices within a limited area like an office, school or home to share resources like printers, files etc.
- WAN: Used to connect LANs over long distances, e.g. the internet which connects networks globally.
- PAN: Used for transferring data between devices in close proximity without an access point, e.g. Bluetooth connecting a phone to wireless headphones.
- MAN: Used to connect networks across an entire city/town, e.g. cable TV networks.
- VPN: Used to provide secure access to a private network over the internet, e.g. for remote employees to access company resources.

<note>Accept any other valid application. Do not award marks for just defining the network type.</note>

parts.content: With reference to Source B, explain the roles of a modem and a network interface in enabling network connectivity.
parts.markscheme:
### Roles of a modem

- Modulates (encodes) digital data from the computer into analog signals $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transmits the analog signals over telephone/cable lines $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Roles of a network interface

- Provides the physical connection between the computer and the network $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Handles the encoding/decoding of data into the appropriate format for transmission over the network $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: With reference to Source D, describe the function of the TCP/IP protocol in network communication.
parts.markscheme:
TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol) is a set of communication protocols used to transmit data over the Internet and other networks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Its main functions are:

- Establishing connections between devices/computers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Breaking data into packets and reassembling them at the destination
- Ensuring reliable data transmission by handling errors, retransmissions, and flow control

<note>No marks for simply stating that TCP/IP is a protocol or set of protocols without describing its function.</note>

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, discuss how network types, components, and protocols facilitate efficient data communication across different environments. Provide examples to support your answer.
parts.markscheme:
### Network Types <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Wired networks (e.g. Ethernet) provide high-speed, reliable data transfer over physical cables
  - Suitable for local area networks (LANs) within buildings/campuses
- Wireless networks (e.g. Wi-Fi, cellular) enable mobility and flexibility
  - Suitable for wide area networks (WANs) covering larger geographical areas

### Network Components <marks>4 marks</marks>

- Routers connect multiple networks and route data between them
  - Use routing protocols (e.g. OSPF, BGP) to determine optimal paths
- Switches connect devices within the same network
  - Use MAC addresses to forward data frames to the correct destination
- Access points provide wireless connectivity for client devices
- Servers store and distribute data/services across the network

### Network Protocols <marks>6 marks</marks>

- TCP/IP protocol suite governs data communication over the Internet
  - TCP ensures reliable, ordered delivery of data packets
  - IP handles logical addressing and routing of packets
- HTTP/HTTPS protocols enable retrieval of web resources
  - HTTP is unsecured, HTTPS uses encryption (e.g. TLS/SSL) for secure communication
- Email protocols like SMTP, POP3, IMAP facilitate email exchange
- VPN protocols (e.g. IPsec, SSL/TLS) create secure, encrypted connections over public networks

<examTip>Provide specific examples of how different network types, components, and protocols are used in various environments (e.g. home, office, mobile) to facilitate efficient data communication.</examTip>

questions.specification:
**Digital currency**

Bitcoin is a type of digital currency (cryptocurrency). This is money that can be sent via the internet and exchanged for goods, services, or money in different currencies. Users can buy bitcoins using real money, and bitcoins can either be spent or stored in a digital wallet for later use. Bitcoins can be used in every country, and sending bitcoins is as simple as sending an email.

A bitcoin wallet is an application that can be installed on a computer or mobile device. Once a bitcoin wallet is installed, the user will get a bitcoin address to use when transferring bitcoins to and from the wallet. This transfer uses private and public key encryption. Users should have a strong password to access their bitcoin wallet.

Bitcoin operates on a peer-to-peer (P2P) network, and users of bitcoins are identified by their bitcoin address. Some users of bitcoins wish to remain anonymous, but this is not always possible.

parts.content:
Bitcoin transactions are made between individuals without the knowledge of banks, governments, or credit card companies. Some governments are investigating whether they should regulate digital transactions, such as those made using bitcoins.

To what extent is it appropriate for governments to regulate digital transactions, such as those made using bitcoins?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   If transactions can be done without disclosing the name of the individuals (anonymity), it could be a way to use money obtained illegally (money laundering –values).
*   Governments will not be able to see all commercial transactions and therefore may miss the possibility to tax commercial activities – informality, lack of transparency.
*   Bitcoin wallet providers may have an agreement with clients not to disclose information – privacy invasion.
*   It is also possible to send a payment without revealing your identity. This allows people to transfer funds across country borders without cost but also allows users to buy illegal products anonymously.
*   Unregulated services, such as bitcoin, do not provide the protection of regulated services, such as banks.
*   For governments to be able to forecast financial trends, they need to have all of the information available. If some information is withheld, it makes forecasting more problematic.
*   There is an ongoing debate between the privacy of the user versus the security of the state. The questions could be reframed as what is an acceptable level of regulation/surveillance?
*   If regulation is too strict, it will stifle innovation and may prevent worthwhile developments in digital currency.
*   Different degrees of regulation by governments of different countries could create an uneven playing field for digital currency transactions.
*   As bitcoin is global, regulation could also impact the rights of people outside the government’s jurisdiction.

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

Keywords: laws, regulations, environment, cryptocurrency, privacy, anonymity, surveillance, change, power, systems, values

parts.content: Define the term ‘bandwidth’ and its importance in network performance.
parts.markscheme:
Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted through a network or internet connection within a certain period of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Bandwidth is important for network performance because it determines how fast data can be transferred. Higher bandwidth allows more data to be transmitted simultaneously, resulting in faster download/upload speeds and better performance for applications that require large amounts of data transfer (e.g. video streaming, online gaming). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the role of encryption in securing network data transmission.
parts.markscheme:
Encryption is the process of encoding data/information in such a way that only authorized parties can access it. Therefore, encryption allows secure data transmission over networks by:

- Converting data into ciphertext that is unreadable to unauthorized parties $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Providing confidentiality/privacy of data during transmission $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Other valid points:

- Preventing unauthorized access/eavesdropping $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Authentication of sender/receiver $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensuring data integrity (data is not modified during transmission) $\rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how interoperability ensures that different devices and systems can communicate on a network.
parts.markscheme:
Interoperability allows different devices and systems to communicate on a network by:

- Having agreed/standardized protocols and data formats <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allowing different hardware and software to connect and exchange data <marks>1 mark</marks>

Examples could include:

- Web browsers being able to display websites from different web servers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Email clients being able to send/receive emails regardless of email provider <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any other valid examples of interoperability</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the importance of security measures, such as firewalls and VPNs, in protecting networks from cyber threats.
parts.markscheme:
Firewalls and VPNs are important security measures for protecting networks from cyber threats:

- Firewalls act as a barrier between a private network and the public internet, filtering incoming and outgoing traffic based on predefined security rules. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - They can block unauthorized access attempts and malicious traffic. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) encrypt data transmitted over public networks, preventing unauthorized access or interception. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - VPNs provide secure remote access for employees or partners to access the company network. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Other important points:

- Firewalls and VPNs are not foolproof, but they significantly reduce the risk of cyber attacks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They should be combined with other security measures like antivirus software, access controls, and regular software updates. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Discuss the specific roles of firewalls and VPNs, and how they complement other security measures for a comprehensive network security strategy.</examTip> <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
**Voice simulation**

Lyrebird voice-cloning software was created by three university students from Montreal. This technology was originally developed to change the lives of people who have lost their voice. These people can use the software to create a new voice by choosing one from samples provided in the software.

However, Lyrebird can also clone voices. Lyrebird claims that it can clone a person’s voice in just 60 seconds. Users only need to access the website, create an account, and generate a voice print by repeating a number of predetermined sentences. Although the cloning is not perfect, the developers of Lyrebird believe that both the time to clone the voice and the quality of the cloning will improve in the future.

The software allows the user to alter the speech and add emotion (such as, anger, elation, and surprise), increasing the ways in which it can be used.

The initial voice print for the cloning process requires the most time and a lot of processing power. However, creating speech from the voice print is a simple process, generating around 1000 sentences almost instantly. The voice print is saved as a compressed audio file.

Lyrebird has considered the ethical issues regarding biometrics\* and acknowledges that it highlights some key societal issues. Lyrebird has taken the decision to make the software available to all in an attempt to address these issues.  
  

* * *

\* biometrics: a means of personal identification through analysis of unique physical traits or behavioural characteristics (for example, through voice patterns or fingerprints)

questions.specification:
**Source A**
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7d7162f2-f79d-4531-afed-2864c786daa3)

**Source B**
Through this article today, we are here to share with you the various challenges in the internet of things.
Security challenges- The first and foremost on the list of challenges is security. As the backbone of IoT is data storage and sharing, the biggest question arises about the security of data. Enabling every small physical object with the feature of sharing information may attract multiple raised brows.
Lack of encryption- Encryption sounds like the ultimate answer to security issues. But hackers may manipulate these algorithms and turn a protective system into the most serious loophole.
Lack of sufficient testing and updating- With the increasing market of IoT, production has to be faster. To compete in the race of production, manufacturers lack tests and updates. The main focus of IoT manufacturers now seems to be production, not security. Thus products lack proper and regular testing and updates. This makes IOTs prone to being attacked by hackers.


**Source C**
A smart home is a household with internet-connected appliances you can remotely control using a tablet or smartphone. It uses smart devices such as smart TVs, smart thermostats, air conditioners, and even a robot vacuum. They are then connected together in a single network, through either hardwired or wireless systems like Zigbee, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and NFC, among others.
Using the Internet of Things (IoT) technology, your smart appliances can communicate and share real time data with each other. This allows the devices to perform scheduled and automated tasks.
IoT home gadgets bounce data back and forth with the use of sensors, learning and processing your patterns to automatically adjust themselves to your comfort. Some smart home Internet of Things applications are automatic light switches, burglar alarms, and voice-activated sound systems.

**Source D** 
A tired business person returns to their certified IoT smart home after a long working week. The smart security system senses they are alone and initiates the “Friday Night In” sequence. An intercom with a thoughtful, comforting voice suggests they might want to order in tonight. The business person unloads their things in the kitchen where the smart stove displays a selection of take-outs, rather than its default recipe guide.
After the food arrives they retreat to the living room to watch some TV. The smart TV prepares a selection of Netflix marathons categorized by mood. They choose: “Looking to be cheered up? Comedy Playlist.”
Before starting the program, they review a set of graphs displaying the data from their activity and diet throughout the day. A list of tips for smart living is generated, one of which reads that based on the number of consecutive nights spent alone, they might consider exploring a selection of popular dating sites instead of watching TV.
With an inadvertent slip of their thumb the request is OK’d and instantly a set of profiles are displayed, each chosen from a generated list of their tracked preferences. A flurry of pings and messages from other stay-at-home hopefuls fills the screen. The smart home intercom exclaims, “You’ve got mail!” The confused and beleaguered business person fumbles for the remote and… uh-oh, the TV snaps a selfie in response to the flood of pings.
Their image, sitting in their underwear eating noodles appears briefly on the screen before being whisked off into the ether. The flood of messages doubles, the system freezes causing the smart home to reboot. The house goes dark.

parts.content: With reference to the sources and your own knowledge, discuss whether the use of IoT in smart homes is an incremental or transformational change.
parts.markscheme:
Define Transformational and Incremental Change <marks>1 mark


**Possible Arguments for Incremental Change**

- IoT in smart homes builds on existing technologies like sensors, automation, and internet connectivity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It enhances convenience and efficiency in homes, but does not fundamentally change the purpose or function of a home <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smart home features can be gradually added or removed without drastically altering the living experience <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Possible Arguments for Transformational Change**

- IoT enables homes to become highly interconnected and responsive to user needs, changing the way we interact with our living spaces <marks>1 mark</marks>
Example: Voice assistants allowing control of various home systems
- It introduces new capabilities like remote monitoring, predictive maintenance, and personalized environments, transforming the concept of a home <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Widespread adoption of smart homes could lead to changes in housing design, energy consumption patterns, and even social norms <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Other Relevant Points**

- The degree of change may depend on the specific IoT applications and the extent of their integration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Early stages of IoT adoption may be incremental, but as technology matures, it could lead to more transformational changes <marks>1 mark</marks>
<examTip>Discuss both perspectives and use relevant examples from the sources and own knowledge to support the arguments</examTip>
<marks>4 marks</marks> for a well-developed, balanced discussion considering multiple perspectives and using relevant examples from the sources and own knowledge.

parts.markscheme:

**Advantages of cloud-based storage:**

*   No need for them to invest in storage infrastructure on site (systems).
*   Data security will be provided by the company responsible for the cloud-based storage.
*   Data backup facilities will be provided by the company responsible for the cloud-based storage (systems).
*   Collaborating and sharing genome data with other data companies or external scientists will be more convenient (systems).
*   Accessibility – having the data on the cloud will enable the _MediResearch s_cientists towork remotely using an internet connection (systems).
*   Scalability – _MediResearch_ can expand or reduce storage capacity subscription on a needs basis (systems).

**Disadvantages of cloud-based storage:**

*   The data may be managed by a third party, which could cause issues linked to its use / security.
*   _MediResearch_ may feel that data security may be more easily compromised.
*   The company will have to ensure that they have enough internet bandwidth for data accessibility.
*   Storing data in the cloud may sometimes be difficult – _MediResearch_’s existing data management may not integrate well with the cloud vendor’s system (systems).
*   Considering the nature and sensitivity of the genome data held by _MediResearch_, the government policies of the country where the cloud storage unit is placed may influence the way data is stored/used or shared (values).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

questions.specification:
**China's social credit scheme**

  
The Chinese government is proposing a social credit scheme that will reward its citizens with points for what it considers to be good behaviour. This can be done by monitoring citizens’ social media connections, purchasing history and location data.

CCTV cameras are widely used in China, and there are concerns that facial recognition technology that uses artificial intelligence (AI) is already being utilized by the government to detect “good” and “bad” behaviours of its citizens. The facial recognition system requires high-resolution images\*.

* * *

\* high-resolution images: images that are of a quality that allows them to be used for printing. Usually this means they are 300 pixels per inch.

parts.content:
Other governments are considering setting up similar systems that will use facial recognition to gather information about the behaviour of its citizens.

Explain why the developers of the new facial recognition system would need both primary **and** secondary data collection.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

*   Primary data collection, such as collecting potential users interacting with the facial recognition system, would provide information about these interactions in real time. Interviews with developers of similar systems would provide rich and nuanced information.
*   Secondary data collection may involve gathering information about existing facial recognition systems and would provide a wider knowledge base for making informed decisions.
*   Combining the real-time interaction of potential users with the facial recognition system and the data obtained from literature reviews will provide the greatest quantity of information and enable the developers to make informed decisions about the best way to develop the facial recognition system.

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a reason why_ _the developers of the new facial recognition system would need both primary and secondary data collection_ _and **\[1\]** for each subsequent development of that reason, up to **\[3\]**._

parts.content: Explain why using high-resolution images could be a challenge to the implementation of a facial recognition system.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

*   High resolution images may require a longer transfer time.
*   Which will require computers with high specifications to be processed in a timely manner.
*   May require considerable storage space.
*   May require lossy compression to transfer them quickly which may lead to a reduction in the quality / usefulness of the image.
*   The cost of purchasing the equipment required to obtain high resolution images.

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a reason why having images with a higher resolution could be a challenge to the implementation of a facial recognition system_ _and **\[1\]** for each subsequent development of that reason up to **\[2\]**._

parts.content: Discuss the advantages **and** disadvantages of a government using a facial recognition system that uses artificial intelligence (AI) to monitor the behaviour of its citizens.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

**Advantages:**

*   May improve people’s behaviour as they will know they are being watched (values, ethics).
*   May stop crime before it happens meaning fewer people in jail or in court (values, ethics, monitoring). Similar to a speeding camera but for people.
*   Might need fewer police to patrol and put themselves in danger (cost).
*   Could help to improve the physical environment by catching those who litter / responsible for polluting the environment.
*   Could allow people to be more accurately identified leading to fewer false accusations/prosecutions (reliability, trust).
*   Allows the government to track and apprehend criminals i.e., makes society safer as criminals are caught (monitoring).
*   AI will be more efficient than manual checking and may learn from its experiences (systems).

**  
Disadvantages:** ·

*   Feeling of being watched will be bad for morale of population (ethics, power, surveillance).
*   May lead to a distrust in the government (trust, values).
*   Might be punished incorrectly (or rewarded incorrectly) if the facial recognition fails (reliability, systems).
*   Criminals might just find other ways of committing crime, such as wearing masks or avoiding the cameras (values).
*   People wear masks (against pollution) or hats which cover their faces (when it is very cold), won’t be recognized that easily (reliability).
*   Invasion of privacy as people are never able to not be under surveillance.
*   May take up considerable resources in developing and maintaining the data and hardware (cost).
*   Facial recognition is not that accurate so there might be many false positives (reliability, systems, accountability).
*   AI may have inherent biases, e.g., it may not recognize particular physical features (values, ethics).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** government, data, algorithm, accuracy, reliability, model, monitoring, surveillance, power, systems, ethics, values, trust, accountability, cost, feasibility

parts.content: Identify two components of the World Wide Web and describe their functions.
parts.markscheme:
### Components of the World Wide Web

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a valid component, such as:
  - Web browser (e.g. Chrome, Firefox, Safari) - used to access and display web pages
  - Web server - stores and serves web pages/content to clients
  - URL (Uniform Resource Locator) - address used to locate web pages
  - HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) - protocol for transmitting data over the web
  - HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) - language used to create web pages

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a valid description of the function of the identified component

<note>Award marks for any other valid and relevant components and their functions</note>

parts.content: Outline the role of URLs in accessing web resources.
parts.markscheme:
URLs (Uniform Resource Locators) play a crucial role in accessing web resources. Here is an outline of their role:

- URLs provide a standardized way to identify and locate resources on the internet <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They specify the protocol (e.g., http, https) to be used to access the resource <marks>1 mark</marks>
- URLs contain the domain name or IP address of the web server hosting the resource <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They include the path to the specific resource (e.g., file, webpage) on the server  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how search engines use web protocols to help users find information.
parts.markscheme:
To access websites, search engines use the HTTP/HTTPS protocols:
- HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is used to request and transmit data over the internet <marks>1 mark</marks>
- HTTPS (HTTP Secure) is the secure version, encrypting the data for privacy <marks>1 mark</marks>

Search engines also use other protocols:
- DNS (Domain Name System) to translate domain names to IP addresses <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{e.g. www.example.com} \rightarrow \text{192.168.0.1}
$$

- Robots.txt file specifies which parts of a website should not be accessed by web crawlers <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the impact of the web on information accessibility, considering both the benefits of easy access and the risks of misinformation.
parts.markscheme:
### Possible Benefits of Easy Access

- Vast amount of information available online from various sources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ability to access information from anywhere with an internet connection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Democratization of knowledge, making information accessible to a wider audience <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Risks of Misinformation

- Lack of quality control or fact-checking for online information <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spread of false or misleading information (e.g., fake news, conspiracy theories) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Difficulty in verifying the credibility and accuracy of online sources <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Balanced Evaluation

- Recognize the advantages of easy access to information for education, research, and personal growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acknowledge the potential dangers of misinformation and the need for critical thinking and fact-checking <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should provide a well-rounded analysis considering both the benefits and risks, and demonstrate an understanding of the impact of the web on information accessibility.</note>

parts.content: Identify the steps used by the voice biometrics technology to authenticate a customer calling _CBR Bank_.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

parts.content:
_CBR Bank_ holds a large amount of information on its customers. Some customers are concerned about the security, privacy and anonymity of their data.

For **each** of the concerns above, explain**one** policy that _CBR Bank_ could use to address the concerns of its customers.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Security:**

*   User access to data is limited to authorized personnel – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   Username and password access is implemented – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   A password policy is implemented (e.g., minimum length, mix of characters, changed after a given number of days etc_._).
*   Two-factor / two-step authentication is implemented (e.g., one-time password/PIN to a mobile phone, code-generating device supplied by the bank, confirmation email).
*   Data is encrypted – to ensure data is secure during transmission.
*   A firewall is used to protect the bank’s server.
*   Bank servers are kept updated with the latest software / security patches.
*   Bank employees are prohibited from accessing customer data on personal devices / devices outside the company network – to ensure that all devices are protected by the bank’s security measures.

**Privacy:**

*   Customers are informed to specify how the data may be used – if/how it may be shared with third parties.
*   Only authorized personnel will have access to the bank database – not all employees will be able to view data.

**Anonymity:**

*   Ensure the customer’s anonymity is maintained – when data is shared with third parties, the data that could give the identity of a customer must be detached.
*   Reports are kept anonymous – reports cannot allow individuals to be identified.

**_N.B.:_** _T__he response requires an explanation of a policy and not a discussion of the problems themselves. There must be a policy for **each** kind of concern: security, privacy and anonymity and reason(s)._

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a policy that CBR Bank could use to address the security, privacy and anonymity concerns of its customers and **\[1\]** for a development of the policy identified up to a maximum of **\[2\]**._

_Mark as **\[2\] + \[2\] + \[2\]**._

parts.content:
The chief executive officer (CEO) of _CBR Bank_, Alice McEwan, said in a recent interview, “_CBR Bank_ will be replacing all passwords, PINs and personal verification questions for our online banking and mobile banking with voice biometrics recognition.”

To what extent are the changes proposed by Alice beneficial for both _CBR Bank_’s customers **and** _CBR Bank_’s IT support?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**For customers:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   Customers don’t have to remember a password or PIN code / may not need additional verification such as one-time codes (automation).
*   It is more secure, as voice characteristics are unique.
*   It is harder for others to hack online banking with voice biometrics recognition (security).
*   Some customers may have physical conditions that make entering PINs/passwords difficult – voice recognition will avoid having to type (access/inclusion).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept foreign accents or a range of voices (systems).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access (security).
*   Someone with very similar voices (e.g., a member of the same family) may be able to gain access to the bank account (security).

**For IT support:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   It is more secure, less likely to be hacked – fewer problems for IT staff to deal with (automation, systems).
*   It is easy for customers to record by themselves – no IT staff required to set up (automation, systems).
*   IT staff do not have to deal with lost password/PIN (automation, systems).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept particular accents – customers cannot access their online banking and will need support (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access – customers may complain about unauthorized access, IT staff will have to investigate hacked accounts (security, values).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult – a greater number of customers might need to call support to access their own account (systems).
*   When the new system is implemented, _CBR_ _Bank_’s IT support could be overwhelmed with overlooked bugs (reliability).
*   Audio files / biometric templates will require more storage space than passwords / PINs – this may make backing up data more time consuming / require IT support to increase available storage space, etc (data).
*   Initial implementation of the new system may require additional IT support staff.
*   IT support staff may face an increased workload (e.g., if the old system initially has to run parallel to the new system).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** security, authentication, stakeholder, reliability, data, security, change, identity, power, systems, values, ethics, accountability, transparency, access, inclusion

parts.content: Outline the role of network protocols, such as TCP/IP, in enabling data exchange.
Explain how routers and switches work together to support connectivity within a network.
parts.markscheme:
- Routers connect multiple networks together and route data packets between them <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Switches connect devices within the same network and forward data packets to the intended destination <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Routers:} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Operate at the network layer (layer 3) of the OSI model} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Use IP addresses to determine where to forward packets} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Connect different networks (e.g., LAN to WAN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Switches:} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Operate at the data link layer (layer 2) of the OSI model} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Use MAC addresses to forward packets within the same network} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Connect devices in the same LAN}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Routers and switches work together to enable communication across networks <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Switches connect devices within a LAN
  - Routers connect LANs to other networks (e.g., the internet)

parts.content: Evaluate the benefits and challenges of using wireless transmission media over wired media in a corporate network.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefits of wireless transmission media

- Mobility/portability <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Users can move around freely with wireless devices
  - Devices are not tethered to a fixed location

- Ease of installation/deployment <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - No need to install cables/wires
  - Wireless networks can be set up quickly

- Cost savings <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Eliminates cabling costs
  - Reduces labor costs for installation

- Scalability/flexibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Easy to expand wireless network coverage
  - Can accommodate changes in office layout/environment

### Challenges of wireless transmission media

- Security risks <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Wireless signals can be intercepted/eavesdropped
  - Vulnerable to unauthorized access/hacking

- Interference/signal degradation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Signals can be disrupted by obstacles, other wireless devices
  - Limited range/coverage compared to wired networks

- Bandwidth limitations <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Wireless networks generally have lower bandwidth than wired
  - May not be suitable for high-bandwidth applications

- Compliance/regulatory issues <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Need to follow wireless regulations/standards
  - Potential legal issues with unauthorized wireless access points

parts.content: To what extent does bandwidth availability impact the quality of online activities like streaming and gaming, considering both user experience and network limitations?
parts.markscheme:
- Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher bandwidth allows for faster data transfer rates and better quality for online activities like:
    - Video streaming (higher resolution, less buffering) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Online gaming (lower latency, smoother gameplay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Video conferencing (higher quality audio/video) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- User experience is impacted by bandwidth limitations:
    - Low bandwidth leads to poor quality video/audio, lag, buffering <marks>1 mark</marks>
    <commonMistake>Confusing bandwidth with internet speed</commonMistake>

- Network limitations that impact bandwidth availability:
    - Type of internet connection (e.g. fiber optic vs DSL) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Distance from internet service provider <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Network congestion during peak usage times <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Shared bandwidth among multiple users/devices <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Solutions to increase bandwidth:
    - Upgrade to a higher-tier internet plan <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Use wired Ethernet instead of WiFi <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Optimize home network (e.g. better router placement) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Answers should consider both the user perspective and technical network factors.</note>

parts.content: Identify two services commonly provided by ISPs and explain their purposes.
parts.markscheme:
### Service 1: Internet Connectivity <marks>1 mark</marks>

- ISPs provide internet access by connecting customers to their network infrastructure $\\ $ and the global internet.
- This allows users to browse websites, send/receive emails, stream content, etc.

### Service 2: Email Services <marks>1 mark</marks>

- ISPs offer email services with email addresses associated with their domain.
- This allows users to send and receive emails using the ISP's email servers.

<note>Award 1 mark for each service identified and its purpose explained correctly. Accept any other reasonable services provided by ISPs.</note>

parts.content: Outline how video conferencing services benefited businesses during the COVID-19 pandemic.
parts.markscheme:
Video conferencing services benefited businesses during the COVID-19 pandemic in the following ways:

- Allowed employees to work remotely/from home while maintaining communication and collaboration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enabled meetings, presentations, and training sessions to be conducted online without physical presence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced travel costs and time associated with in-person meetings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitated international/global collaboration and communication despite travel restrictions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how VOIP services allow for cost-effective communication over the internet.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following points up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>:

- Voice over IP (VoIP) converts analog voice signals into digital data packets
- These data packets are transmitted over the internet/IP network
- This allows voice communication to be transmitted over existing internet infrastructure
- Avoiding the need for traditional telephone networks/infrastructure reduces costs

<commonMistake>
Candidates may confuse VoIP with video conferencing or other internet communication services.
</commonMistake>

<examTip>
Ensure you explain how VoIP specifically allows for cost savings compared to traditional telephony.
</examTip>

parts.content: Evaluate the impact of ISP-provided services on the flexibility and productivity of remote work, including both benefits and limitations.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefits of ISP-provided services for remote work flexibility and productivity

- Reliable high-speed internet connectivity enables seamless access to cloud-based applications, video conferencing, and file sharing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ISPs may offer virtual private network (VPN) services for secure remote access to company networks and resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bundled communication services like VoIP telephony facilitate remote collaboration and communication <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Limitations of ISP-provided services for remote work

- Dependence on ISP for internet availability and quality of service, potential for outages or throttling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Data caps or usage limits on internet plans may restrict bandwidth-intensive activities like video calls <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limited technical support or customer service from ISPs for troubleshooting remote work issues <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potential security risks if ISP-provided VPN or remote access solutions are not properly configured or maintained <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Geographic availability and pricing of ISP services may vary, impacting remote work feasibility in certain areas <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the ethical implications of user data collection and surveillance on social networks, focusing on privacy, security, and consent.
parts.markscheme:
### Privacy and consent <marks>4 marks</marks>
- User data collection often happens without explicit consent or transparency
- Privacy concerns over personal data being collected, shared, and used for purposes not intended by users
- Lack of control over how personal data is used and disseminated
- Potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information

### Security risks <marks>3 marks</marks>
- Collection of user data creates security vulnerabilities and risks of hacking/data breaches
- Centralized data storage makes platforms an attractive target for cyber attacks
- Stolen data can be used for identity theft, fraud, or other malicious activities
$$
\text{Risk} \propto \text{Amount of data collected}
$$

### Surveillance and social control <marks>3 marks</marks>
- User data enables mass surveillance and monitoring of online activities
- Potential for data to be used for social control, manipulation, or discrimination
- Chilling effects on free speech and expression due to perceived monitoring
- Asymmetry of power between platforms with user data and individual users

### Ethical considerations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Lack of transparency and informed consent violates ethical principles
- User autonomy and right to privacy are undermined
- Utilitarian arguments about benefits must be weighed against ethical costs
- Need for stronger data protection laws and user-centric data governance models

questions.specification:
**Technology disruption in Orams Academy**

Many schools use virtual learning environments (VLEs), such as Firefly, PowerSchool Learning, Schoology, Final Site LMS, and Google Classroom. The VLE is used for document storage, lesson content, and homework management as well as for communication between the school, students and parents.

In addition to the VLE, collaborative cloud-based online tools, such as Google G Suite and Microsoft Office 365, are used by students and teachers.

Recently, Daniela Stark, the principal at Orams Academy, has become concerned that using only one VLE may not prove to be as beneficial as originally thought. She has heard that some teachers and students are also successfully using social media tools, such as Facebook, Twitter and Instagram, to share their learning. She has also been told that the lack of bandwidth that the school experiences at times can cause problems.

Daniela is considering an alternative approach, allowing teachers and students to choose the most appropriate tools to manage their learning.

parts.content:
The increasing use of cloud-based applications and online collaborative tools has led to Orams Academy introducing an acceptableuse policy for students and teachers.

Explain**three** reasons why Orams Academy decided to introduce an acceptable use policy.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   To establish a philosophy of Orams Academy relating to the use of online environments…
*   which acts as a guiding statement and provides the overarching principles for online behaviour (values).  
    

*   To establish a code of conduct (values)…
*   that provides clear and unambiguous directions about how staff and students should act while online / using appropriate language in text chats / avoiding bullying behaviour / avoiding copying or plagiarism.  
      
    

*   To outline sanctions…
*   that would be imposed if staff or students did not adhere to the code of conduct (values).  
      
    

*   To establish a disclaimer…
*   so that Orams Academy is not responsible for the actions of the staff or students if an illegal activity is carried out / managers at Orams Academy are able to supervise everything that is posted onto the VLE / managers at Orams Academy would be able to read students’ messages (spaces).  
      
    

*   To establish data privacy and protection guidelines (security)…
*   so it will be transparent who owns the data created in the VLE, for how long, and what type of data is collected by the VLE servers (systems).

_Award **\[1\]** for each reason given and an additional **\[1\]** for a development of this reason up to a maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

parts.content: Discuss whether Daniela should make every teacher at Orams Academy use the same learning platform **or** allow each teacher to choose their own preferred learning management approach.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**A single learning platform:**

*   This means that all of the information is centralized and can be easily accessed by staff, students and parents (systems).
*   The training requirements for staff are reduced, as they only have to use one system (systems).
*   It prevents the use of a range of formats that may be incompatible, meaning that resources cannot be shared, or may need adapting to work on a different platform (systems).
*   Having a common learning platform would make it convenient for the IT department to take backup/restore and roll over data to new academic year (systems).
*   It makes it easier for senior managers to monitor the actions of staff, view lessons, etc., for consistency (values, power).
*   Can be integrated into any acceptable use policy or training requirements (values).
*   May be costly and based on proprietary software, meaning that fixes could be expensive and take a long time to happen (change).
*   If the single platform is inaccessible for any reason, all learning content would be inaccessible (systems).

**Teachers choosing their own preferred learning management approach:**

*   allows teachers to use their expertise of particular tools
*   allows teachers to feel they are being allowed to use their creativity and innovative approach to the teaching and learning (power)
*   may integrate with social media tools that may be blocked by the school and provide a better user experience
*   may provide better functionality than a “one size fits all” approach
*   may lead to staff or students working outside an acceptable use policy / using the sites inappropriately
*   the training requirements for staff will be increased if the staff are unfamiliar with the platforms / training will only occur on an _ad hoc_ basis / training might be decreased since staff are already familiar with the tools they choose to use
*   expertise of a platform may depend on particular teachers – if they leave, then no one may be able to support students with this tool (systems).

**Keywords:** business, learning, education, training, judgement, platform, monitoring, acceptable use policy, interoperability, policy, change, systems, values

### Source A: The Evolution and Impact of Different Types of Computers
Computers have diversified significantly, ranging from large-scale mainframes that handle intensive processing tasks to wearable devices like fitness trackers that support everyday activities. Each type serves unique functions across industries.

For example, mainframe computers power large databases and transactions in banking, processing millions of transactions in seconds. Smart devices, such as mobile phones, have also transformed digital communication, offering constant connectivity to the internet and applications.

### Source B: Components of a Computer: Hardware, Interfaces, and Software
A computer’s functionality relies on the interplay of hardware, interfaces, and software. The central processing unit (CPU) acts as the “brain” of the system, while memory stores data temporarily and storage components hold data permanently. User interfaces enable interaction, and software executes tasks and provides structure.

For instance, a smartphone’s CPU works in tandem with its operating system and touchscreen to enable fast user responses and efficient multitasking, providing a seamless experience in everyday tasks.


### Source C: Computer Coding and Programming Languages
Programming languages allow humans to communicate instructions to computers. From foundational languages like Fortran and C to modern languages like Python and JavaScript, each has specific rules and structures. Coding’s flexibility allows it to be applied in diverse fields, from software development to artificial intelligence.

For instance, Python’s readability has made it a popular choice in data science, allowing researchers to analyze data and create machine learning algorithms effectively.


### Source D: The Evolution of Computing: From First Generation to Quantum Computing

Sample:
### Introduction

The evolution of computers—from mainframes to quantum technology—has drastically impacted industries, enhancing productivity, accuracy, and innovation across various sectors.

### Industry Examples

#### Finance

Mainframe computers support extensive data processing, allowing banks to conduct high-frequency transactions and manage customer databases efficiently. Advanced algorithms further enable real-time trading, risk assessment, and fraud detection.

#### Healthcare

The rise of wearable computers has enabled continuous patient monitoring, allowing healthcare providers to gather data on vital signs and activity levels for better patient care. In addition, advancements in machine learning and Python applications support diagnostic tools that analyze patient data, aiding in early detection and treatment planning.

### Technological Evolution and Impact

#### Generational Improvements

The shift from transistors to microprocessors and, potentially, quantum computing has led to faster, more efficient devices, opening up new possibilities for automation, data analysis, and machine learning.

#### Programming and Software

The evolution of programming languages (e.g., from Fortran to Python) has expanded accessibility, enabling industries to develop more complex applications tailored to their needs, such as AI-driven diagnostics in healthcare and financial forecasting tools.

### Conclusion

The continuous evolution of computing technology has enabled industries to streamline operations, achieve higher accuracy, and unlock new innovation areas, demonstrating the transformative power of computer advancement on modern business and society.

questions.specification:
**Source A**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/68fc2d2e-593d-4a63-83eb-d194006f2ecf)

**Source B (IslaVerde self-description excerpt)**

IslaVerde’s clinic relies on video consultations with specialists on the mainland. The clinic’s policy document lists minimum network requirements: stable upload speed for video, low packet loss for medical images, and encrypted connections for patient confidentiality. The clinic currently uses fibre as its primary connection and keeps a satellite link as backup for outages. Staff report that when fibre fails, the satellite link keeps messaging and record access running, but video quality often drops and consultations take longer. The policy notes that home patients often connect via mobile hotspots, which can be affected by congestion and data caps. The clinic wants to expand remote monitoring (wearables sending daily measurements), but worries that inconsistent connectivity will create unequal care and that more connected devices will increase cybersecurity risks.

**Source C (data usage statistics from IslaVerde)**  

Over 12 months, fibre uptime averaged 99.1%; there were 6 fibre outages, including one lasting 11 hours.

During fibre outages, satellite enabled basic services, but average consultation duration increased from 18 minutes to 31 minutes.

Mobile hotspot use for home consultations increased by 44%; dropout rates are 3× higher on mobile than on fibre.

Security log summary: 23 blocked intrusion attempts on the clinic gateway; 0 confirmed breaches, but 2 incidents involved staff reusing weak passwords on remote access.

Patient survey: 26% report postponing at least one appointment due to “internet problems,” concentrated in outer hamlets.

**Source D (published letter to IslaVerde)**

The clinic’s connectivity plan treats the internet like a neutral utility: if you have “a link,” you have care. But networks are not interchangeable pipes. Latency, congestion, data caps, and outages shape who can be seen by a doctor and when—and they shape how stressful that care becomes. Pushing remote monitoring sounds modern, yet it expands surveillance-like data collection and widens the gap between households with stable connections and those relying on fragile mobile coverage. Security is also social, not just technical. A single reused password can undermine expensive infrastructure, especially when staff are pressured to “make it work” during outages. IslaVerde should be cautious about building critical health services on connectivity that residents do not control. Resilience is not only about backup links; it is also about maintaining non-digital alternatives and ensuring that network inequality does not become health inequality.

**Answer points**


* Source C quantifies reliability and impact, reporting 99.1 percent uptime, six fibre outages, longer consultations on satellite, and higher dropouts on mobile, while Source D explains why networks differ by identifying latency, congestion, data caps, and outages as factors shaping access to care. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C presents cybersecurity data including 23 blocked intrusion attempts and two password reuse incidents, while Source D stresses that security is socio-technical and that pressure during outages leads to weaker practices. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources highlight benefits such as services continuing through backup links and expanded access to remote healthcare. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources warn about inequality, with Source C noting appointment postponements concentrated in outer hamlets and Source D arguing that network inequality becomes health inequality. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C provides statistical evidence of performance impacts, while Source D offers a critical perspective on the limits of treating networks as neutral utilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C shows concrete performance differences between fibre, satellite, and mobile connections, while Source D argues that networks are not interchangeable pipes and affect who can access care. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for points that clearly compare and contrast the two sources.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per relevant similarity or difference.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses focused on only one source can receive a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>. To reach full marks, responses must address both sources and identify both similarities and differences. Points that describe one source without linking to the other should receive limited credit.</note>


**Sample answer**

Source C focuses on measurable reliability and impact, reporting high fibre uptime, a small number of outages, longer consultation times on satellite connections, and higher dropout rates on mobile networks.  

In contrast, Source D explains why these differences exist by highlighting factors such as latency, congestion, data caps, and outages that shape who can access care. Both sources recognise benefits including continuity of services through backup links and expanded access to remote care. However, they also warn about inequality. Source C shows postponements concentrated in outer hamlets, while Source D argues more broadly that unequal networks translate into health inequality. Overall, Source C provides statistical evidence of performance differences, whereas Source D offers a critical interpretation of networks as non-neutral systems that affect access differently.

parts.content: To what extent should IslaVerde expand internet-dependent healthcare services (such as video consultations and remote monitoring)? Answer this with reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course. Consider how networks enable and constrain participation, reliability and resilience, cybersecurity and privacy, and the ways connectivity can create new digital divides.
parts.markscheme:
**To what extent (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Networks enable healthcare by supporting video consultations that expand specialist access to remote patients. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source B shows that fibre uptime of 99.1 percent supports the feasibility of internet-dependent healthcare. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Outages and degraded satellite performance limit equivalence of care, as consultation times increase from 18 to 31 minutes during fibre failures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Mobile dropout rates three times higher than fibre and postponements affecting 26 percent of patients demonstrate a digital divide in access. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that latency, congestion, data caps, and outages make networks not interchangeable, shaping who can access care. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Backup links provide resilience but require service design for degraded modes such as asynchronous alternatives. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D notes that resilience should also include maintaining non-digital alternatives rather than relying solely on backup links. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Encryption supports confidentiality, but Source C shows ongoing cybersecurity risks through 23 blocked intrusion attempts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Password reuse incidents illustrate social factors where staff pressure undermines technical security measures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Remote monitoring through wearables expands care but introduces surveillance-like data collection and privacy concerns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Network inequality widens gaps between households with stable connections and those dependent on mobile coverage. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Expansion should proceed cautiously with safeguards including offline alternatives, equitable access plans, authentication training, and privacy governance. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to <marks>12 marks</marks> for a balanced evaluative judgement that considers enablement, constraints, resilience, cybersecurity, privacy, and digital divides.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per relevant evaluative point grounded in the sources and course knowledge.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses using only source material or only own knowledge can receive a maximum of <marks>8 marks</marks>. Full marks require synthesis of sources with relevant own knowledge and a balanced judgement on expanding internet-dependent healthcare.</note>


**Sample answer**


Internet-dependent healthcare on IslaVerde should be expanded, but only with appropriate safeguards in place. Networks enable healthcare by allowing video consultations that connect remote patients with specialists. Source B shows that high fibre uptime supports the feasibility of these services under normal conditions.  

However, constraints limit equivalence of care. Source C shows that outages and reliance on satellite links increase consultation times, while mobile connections suffer much higher dropout rates and lead to postponed appointments for many patients. Source D reinforces this by arguing that networks are not interchangeable, as latency, congestion, and data caps shape who can realistically access care.  

Backup links improve resilience, but Source D emphasises that resilience should also include non-digital alternatives. Cybersecurity and privacy concerns remain significant. Although encryption is used, Source C reports blocked intrusion attempts and password reuse, showing that security is partly social. Remote monitoring may expand care but also raises surveillance and privacy concerns.  

Overall, expanding digital healthcare can improve access, but without offline alternatives, equitable access planning, staff training, and strong privacy governance, it risks deepening existing health inequalities alongside digital divides.

questions.specification:
### Source A: Network Security: Protecting Data and Devices
Network security includes tools like firewalls, encryption, and authentication. Firewalls block unauthorized access, while encryption secures data in transit, and authentication verifies user identities. These security measures are essential in corporate and personal networks alike.

For instance, a firewall prevents unauthorized access to a corporate network, and encryption protocols like SSL protect sensitive data during online transactions.



### Source B: Network Services and Applications: From Email to Remote Access
Networks support essential services like email, web browsing, file sharing, and remote access. These services allow data exchange across global networks, supporting both professional and personal communication and collaboration.

For example, SMTP facilitates email transmission, while VPNs enable secure remote access to organizational resources.


### Source C: Understanding Network Addressing: IP and MAC Addresses
Devices on networks are identified by unique IP addresses (for network-level identification) and MAC addresses (for hardware-level identification). Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) assigns IP addresses automatically, while Domain Name System (DNS) translates domain names into IP addresses.

For instance, DNS translates “www.example.com” into an IP address, directing user requests to the appropriate web server.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7e16cb03-871e-4b58-87bc-d771fa273c29)

### Source D: Infrastructure of the Internet: Routers, Switches, and Access Points
The internet relies on routers to direct traffic between networks, switches to connect devices within LANs, and access points to enable Wi-Fi connections. These elements form the backbone of both small networks and the global internet infrastructure.

For example, an office setup includes switches to connect computers, a router to manage internet access, and access points for wireless device connections.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c1f01307-1a1c-4543-8c05-c1f1a469eb65)

questions.specification:
**Social media and political tension**

National governments sometimes block websites at a time of political tension. One example was in 2011, when a government prevented its citizens’ access to _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. Images on these websites were considered inappropriate, with the potential to cause hatred, violence and political unrest in that country. The national government gave direct orders to the internet service providers (ISPs) to block access to these sites for four hours. The aim was to give enough time for site officials to remove the offending images from their websites.

Despite these attempts by the government, some citizens were still able to access these images using proxy servers.

Many people, such as academics, believe that this approach is not appropriate and that governments should focus on the education of their citizens so that they are able to make informed decisions about how to react to potentially offensive information on websites and social media.

parts.content:
Many schools block access to social networking websites like _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. However, other schools are investigating two different options:

*   Monitoring the network to view what websites the students are viewing.
*   Giving different ages of students different levels of access to social media sites.

Analyse these **two** options.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Monitoring the network:**

*   It prevents cyberbullying – keep students safe (values).
*   It teaches students responsibility – digital citizenship (values).
*   It protects students from sharing inappropriate information and images about themselves (values).
*   It identifies any objectionable/inappropriate content that students can access on these sites.
*   It gathers data/information for future policies for the use (or not) of social media.
*   Students of all ages would have access to the same websites, regardless of age – younger students could have access to objectionable/inappropriate content for their age.
*   Privacy of students – school would have access to see what the students are viewing.

**Giving different ages different levels of access:**

*   Some websites have age restrictions – should only be accessed if age appropriate (values).
*   Younger students should not be exposed to objectionable/inappropriate content that is acceptable for older students.
*   Schools have a responsibility to ensure the use of the network is appropriate to various ages.
*   Younger students may not be able to differentiate between true intentions of online friendships (expression).
*   Older students need to learn responsible digital citizenship and take responsibility for their actions (values, trust).
*   Awareness of the benefits and drawbacks of social media sites is gained as the student matures (values).
*   Blocking sites by age could cause issues – blocking content that is needed by older students granting different levels of access may be more cost-effective than implementing an ongoing monitoring system (power, values).

  
**Keywords:** home, online, social, relationships, responsibility, trust, software applications, apps, objectionable content, change, expression, identity, power, values, ethics

parts.content:
Many citizens have raised concerns about the surveillance of their web browsing history or the censorship of selected websites by their national government.

To what extent is it appropriate for national governments to use surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is acceptable:**

*   It can keep children from being victims of cyberbullying, sex trafficking and pornography, objectionable/inappropriate content.
*   It can control/monitor various illegal activities (values).
*   It helps strengthen national security with laws against hacking and impose large fines and punishments (values).
*   It can lessen the incidence of identity theft.
*   Governments may be best informed to determine what may, or may not, be appropriate for their citizens (values).

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is not acceptable:**

*   It removes citizens’ freedom of expression.
*   It can be used to keep information from citizens that may infringe on their privacy and/or anonymity (trust).
*   It can be expensive for the government or may require too many resources for the benefits it brings (systems).
*   It may accidentally block sites that should not be blocked (systems).
*   Knowledge is power.
*   It can be used to identify people who oppose the government (values, expression).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** Automation, connectivity, change, power, systems, values, ethics, machine learning, accountability, transparency, autonomy, surveillance

parts.content: Evaluate the potential impact of quantum computing on industries reliant on data processing, such as cryptography and healthcare.
parts.markscheme:
Potential impacts:

- Quantum computing could dramatically increase computational power, allowing for solving complex problems in seconds that would take traditional computers years or be practically impossible <marks>2 marks</marks>

- In cryptography, quantum computers could break many modern encryption methods by factoring large numbers efficiently, rendering current encryption methods obsolete <marks>2 marks</marks>
$$
\text{Impact on cryptography} = \text{Increased vulnerability of encrypted data}
$$

- In healthcare, quantum computing could enable rapid analysis of massive genomic datasets, leading to breakthroughs in areas like personalized medicine and drug discovery <marks>2 marks</marks>

Limitations:

- Quantum computers are extremely expensive to build and maintain, limiting accessibility for most industries currently <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Technical challenges like error correction and scalability issues need to be overcome before practical applications <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Quantum computing is still in its infancy, so its full potential and limitations are yet to be realized in various industries.</note>

**Schools told not to use cloud computing software applications**

  
Schools in the German state of Hesse will no longer be able to use cloud computing services with software applications, such as G Suite and Office 365, due to new data protection rules. This has identified the risk of schools’ data being stored and accessed by third parties from outside of Germany.

In contrast, many schools in other parts of the world use cloud computing services and software applications. These applications allow students to access software that was previously only installed on their computers.
